Full Terms & Conditions of access and use can be found at
https://www.tandfonline.com/action/journalInformation?journalCode=wshc20
Social Work in Health Care
ISSN: (Print) (Online) Journal homepage: https://www.tandfonline.com/loi/wshc20
Fintech and medical expenses: What social
workers should know
Mathieu Despard, Sally A. Hageman & Maudia Ahmad
To cite this article: Mathieu Despard, Sally A. Hageman & Maudia Ahmad (2023) Fintech
and medical expenses: What social workers should know, Social Work in Health Care, 62:2-4,
93-106, DOI: 10.1080/00981389.2023.2191656
To link to this article:  https://doi.org/10.1080/00981389.2023.2191656
Published online: 26 Mar 2023.
Submit your article to this journal 
Article views: 80
View related articles 
View Crossmark data

Fintech and medical expenses: What social workers should 
know
Mathieu Despard , PhD, MSW
a, Sally A. Hageman , PhD, MSW
b, 
and Maudia Ahmad , MSW, LCSW, LCAS-A, CCMc
aDepartment of Social Work, University of North Carolina, Greensboro, North Carolina, United States; 
bSociology, Social Work and Criminology, Idaho State University, Pocatello, Idaho, United States; 
cSociology and Social Work, North Carolina State Agriculture & Technology University, Greensboro, 
North Carolina, United States
ABSTRACT
Individuals who cannot afford out of pocket medical expenses 
may reduce health care use, resulting in poorer health out­
comes. To ease the situation, employers turn to financial tech­
nology (“fintech”) health care credit applications. We examine 
whether an employer-sponsored credit fintech application 
(MedPut) helps employees manage medical expenses. Results 
of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and probit regression mod­
els reveal MedPut users did worse financially and delayed health 
care due to cost more often than employees who did not use 
MedPut. Results may inform social work policy and direct prac­
tice perspectives on fin-tech and medical expenses.
ARTICLE HISTORY 
Received 30 July 2022  
Accepted 8 March 2023 
KEYWORDS 
Medical debt; financial 
technology; out-of-pocket 
costs; medical credit; health 
care use
The United States (U.S.) has a unique system of financing health care in which 
employers, employees, other individuals, and government pay into various 
public and private insurance plans that finance care. Over half of individuals 
have health insurance through their employer. Plans offered by employers vary 
widely concerning employees’ out-of-pocket (OOP) expenses including pre­
miums, co-payments, deductibles, and co-insurance. More than half (59%) of 
all employees in the U.S. are covered by a high deductible health plan (HDHP) 
through their employer (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022a). These plans are 
attractive to employers because premiums are typically lower than for other 
forms of employer-sponsored insurance. HDHPs require that employees incur 
OOP up to the deductible amount at least $1,400 for an individual and $2,800 
for a family (HealthCare.gov, 2022) before the plan kicks in, unless their 
employer also funds health reimbursement accounts (HRAs) or health savings 
accounts (HSAs).
Lower-paid employees enrolled in HDHPs without employer HRA or HSA 
contributions might find it hard to come up with the cash to pay OOP health 
care expenses until they reach their deductible (Bernstein, 2014). This 
CONTACT Sally A. Hageman 
sallyhageman@isu.edu 
Sociology, Social Work and Criminology, Idaho State 
University, Kegel Liberal Arts Building, Room 350, 921 S., 8th Avenue, Stop 8114, Pocatello, Idaho 83209, United States
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE                        
2023, VOL. 62, NOS. 2–4, 93–106 
https://doi.org/10.1080/00981389.2023.2191656
© 2023 Taylor & Francis Group, LLC 

challenge is even greater among employees who already have unpaid health­
care bills. Recognizing these challenges, some employers have offered employ­
ees MedPut, a digital financial application that helps employees pay medical 
bills and other health care costs using interest-free payment plans and bill 
negotiation services. The purpose of this study was to examine the registration 
choice, use of, and benefits from using MedPut among employees enrolled in 
HDHPs. Accordingly, our research questions are as follows:
(1) What were the registration and usage rates for MedPut? Did these rates 
vary based on employee and employer characteristics?
(2) What were the frequency, amount, and disposition of health care bills 
submitted for payment? Did bill submission vary based on employee 
characteristics?
(3) What were the experiences of employees in paying for health care? To 
what degree do employees put off health care due to cost concerns? Did 
these experiences differ among MedPut users and non-users?
Findings from this study may inform employer perspectives on health 
insurance and other benefits and policy concerning the intersection of health 
care financing, consumer financial health, and health outcomes. Social work­
ers may advocate to improve employer health insurance policy and health care 
financing options to better protect financially vulnerable populations.
Review of the literature
Prior research draws a connection between individuals’ OOP expenses and 
their health care use (Al Rowas et al., 2017; Brot-Goldberg et al., 2017; 
Wharam et al., 2018). These studies found that individuals are sensitive to 
changes in OOP expenses and reduce health care consumption when these 
expenses increase. Another area of research draws a link between medical debt 
and lessened health care use (Bennett & Dismuke, 2010; Doty et al., 2005; 
Wiltshire et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). When confronted with high deduc­
tibles and existing medical debt, individuals’ reductions in health care use may 
have downstream negative effects; they may avoid preventive care or put off 
receiving care for a chronic illness and risk experiencing a catastrophic and 
expensive care event.
Difficulties affording OOP healthcare expenses may result in accumulating 
medical debt; Himmelstein et al. (2019) found that nearly 60% of individuals 
filing for bankruptcy cited medical expenses as a key reason. Difficulty repay­
ing medical debt may render individuals reluctant to seek additional care 
because it would add to an already unmanageable debt burden (Doty et al.,  
2005).
94
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

Individuals have an array of choices: personal savings, HRAs (if offered by 
their employer), HSAs, Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs), credit cards, 
unsecured bank loans, hospital and health care provider charity care discounts 
and payment plans, and payday loans and similar high-interest rate credit 
products. Yet Seymour (2020) found that most individuals with cancer used 
their personal savings to cover medical bills but were still unable to avoid 
medical debt. Peter et al. (2016) found that individuals who used a HDHP 
paired with an HSA saved more but reduced their use of health care services 
compared to respondents who were enrolled in a full coverage health care 
plan. Kalousova and Burgard (2013) found a positive association between 
credit card debt, medical debt and delaying health care services regardless of 
socioeconomic and health differences. These findings suggest that as debt 
rises, health care use falls.
The focus of our study is on a fintech product meant to help workers in 
HDHPs better afford healthcare. Fintech offers access to new types of financial 
products and services that may benefit individuals and families. Digital and 
mobile payment platforms make it easier for people to conduct transactions 
and send and receive money from family members and friends, personal 
financial management apps make it easier to track spending, and savings 
apps can help people build emergency savings via goal setting, transaction 
round-ups, and automatic saving (Despard, in press). A review of over 60 
studies of fintech showed that the use of fintech is correlated with improve­
ments in spending, loan repayment, and saving behaviors and can improve 
access to credit, yet overall evidence on the benefits of fintech is mixed and it is 
particularly unclear whether fintech benefits lower-income individuals and 
families (Despard, in press). In our study, MedPut may have given workers an 
affordable source of credit to pay overdue medical bills and some relief 
through bill reductions. A fintech credit product like MedPut is especially 
important for workers who have low or no credit scores and cannot access 
affordable credit (Atkinson, 2020; Urban Institute, 2021). In describing results 
from our study of MedPut, we can help shed light on ways in which fintech 
may help workers address problems paying for and utilizing healthcare.
Methods
This study was part of a project called the Financial Solutions Lab (FSL) led by 
the Financial Health Network, a national nonprofit. The Lab tests different 
ways to promote financial health using digital technologies. We partnered with 
the Financial Health Network to study digital financial products and services 
offered through the workplace as part of the 5th cohort of the Lab.
For this study, we examined one of these products, MedPut, a digital 
financial application that helps employees pay medical bills and other health 
care costs using interest-free payment plans and bill negotiation services. The 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
95

monthly fee to have MedPut is paid by the employer, the employee, or 
a combination of the two. The employer-paid fee is $2 to $6 per month per 
registered employee, depending on the financing limit, which is from $1,000 to 
$3,000. The employee-paid fee is $3 to $7 per month. MedPut focuses on 
employees enrolled in HDHPs who may need help paying for health care 
expenses until they meet their deductible. Employees register to use the 
MedPut app and upload the health care bills they need help paying. MedPut 
pays the bills after trying to negotiate discounts for the employee. Employees 
make monthly payments via payroll deductions. The amount that employees 
can borrow is limited by monthly repayments capped at 5% of gross pay. 
Repayment terms vary from one month to two years.
Data and sample
Data for this study came from two sources. First, administrative data from the 
MedPut platform was de-identified and shared with researchers for analysis. 
Data were included for 4,317 employees in 21 companies and organizations 
who were invited to use MedPut from January 2018 through July 2020. Data 
included employees’ age, salary range, employer size and industry, and health 
care bills submitted for payment. Second, employees who were registered for 
MedPut were invited by MedPut to complete a survey on two occasions, 
including 120 employees who completed a survey in November 2019 and 
104 employees who completed the same survey in June 2020. A total of 35 
employees completed both surveys. Survey questions asked about employees’ 
experiences with medical bills and debt, use of health care, general household 
financial health, and demographic characteristics. Because MedPut provided 
de-identified administrative and survey data to the authors for analysis, the 
Institutional Review Board at a university in the Midwest determined the 
project to be not human subjects research.
Variables
Dependent variables included whether employees registered for the MedPut 
app, whether registered employees used the app at least once, the number, 
amount, and disposition of submitted bills – current, delinquent, or advance 
payment, and whether a discount was negotiated and the amount of this 
discount. These dependent variables were measured using MedPut adminis­
trative data. Independent variables measured from MedPut administrative 
data included age, job tenure, salary, fee type (self, employer, or shared fee), 
employer size, employer industry, and any dependents in the home.
Additional dependent variables were measured from a survey that MedPut 
users completed concerning financial difficulties and use of MedPut. Financial 
difficulty variables included having problems paying medical bills and being 
96
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

contacted by a collections agency within the prior 12 months, and ability to 
cover household expenses. Also, participants were asked whether in the prior 
six months they had lost a job or taken a pay cut due to illness or injury, 
postponed getting medical or dental care, and/or postponed filling 
a prescription medication because they could not afford it.
Analysis
To answer Research Question 1 concerning variation in MedPut registration 
and use, we estimated the marginal effects of and predicted probabilities 
associated with independent variables on the registration choice using probit 
regression models with covariance control, robust standard errors, and 
employer as a cluster variable. Covariates used in probit models included 
age, salary group, any dependents in the home, MedPut fee type, industry, 
and employer size. To answer Research Questions 2 and 3, we used univariate 
descriptive statistics and bivariate analysis including chi square tests for 
categorical and ordinal level independent variables (e.g., salary group, indus­
try) and t-tests and analysis of variance (ANOVA) for age and job tenure.
Results
Sample description
The mean age of employees who had access to MedPut was 42.79 (SD = 12.69). 
Most (73.20%) employees worked for a large company and the most common 
type of industry was white collar. Over a third of employees had salaries below 
$40,000 and had dependents. Out of 4,317 employees who were invited, 1,234 
or 28.5% registered for MedPut which meant they could submit health care 
bills for payment. Among registrants (N = 1,234), 21.31% used MedPut at least 
once during the study period of 2.5 years. Table 1 provides a full description of 
the sample.
Differences in MedPut registration and use
Marginal effects estimated from probit regression predicting MedPut registration 
indicated that employees with dependents were more than three times as likely to 
register for MedPut (54.0%) compared to employees without dependents (13.8%) 
(p < .01). Employees in small companies or organizations were twice as likely to 
register (68.5%) as employees in medium (29.8%) and large (22.5%) companies or 
organizations (p < .001). Other factors that were statistically significant predictors 
of MedPut registration included lesser job tenure, when employees paid the full fee 
for MedPut, and employees working in blue collar industries. Details of the health 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
97

care financing circumstances of MedPut users and non-uses is provided in 
Table 2.
Marginal effects estimated from probit regression predicting usage 
among employees who registered for MedPut, indicated that employees 
with dependents were more likely to use MedPut (23.7%) compared to 
employees without dependents (15.8%) (p < .05). Employees in blue collar 
industries were more likely to use MedPut (32.4%) than employees in the 
service sector (15.9%) (p < .05), while employees who paid the full fee for 
MedPut were more likely to use it (25.7%) than employees whose

* p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.
98
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

employer paid the full cost (13.3%) (p < .05). MedPut usage rates are 
listed in Table 3.
Number, disposition, and amount of medical bills submitted
Most employees (82%) who used MedPut did so only once; 15% used it twice, 
and the remaining 3% used it three or more times; employees submitted a total 
of 485 bills through 263 opened MedPut cases. Most cases1 were for delinquent 
accounts (77%), followed by current bills (20%), and advance payments (3%) – 
such as when a provider requires an upfront deposit. Of the cases for delin­
quent accounts, 21% were for accounts in collections, which means the bill had 
been assigned or sold by the health care provider to a third party debt 
collection agency due to nonpayment. Results from regression models indi­
cated that no factors (e.g., age) had a statistically significant relationship with 
total bills submitted or with bill disposition.
The mean amount of medical bills submitted through MedPut was 
$1,378.96 (SD = $1,032.09) with a range of $44 to $4,958 and a median of 
$1,066.60. Results from a regression model predicting bill amount indicated 
that an increase in one year of age was associated with $15 more in submitted 
bills (p < .05), yet no other factors were statistically significant.
Negotiated bill reductions
Over a third (37%) of submitted bills were negotiated downward with health 
care providers or collection agencies, saving employees an average of $117 per 
case, with a range of $0 to $2,728. Among cases with successfully negotiated 
reductions, the average discount was 18%. Bills that were reduced were higher 
Table 3. Marginal effects: MedPut usage rate.

Note: Results are from probit regression using marginal effects where dy/dx is the percentage point change 
in probability of the MedPut usage rate based on a one-unit change in the independent variable.
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
99

in their original amount ($1,749) than bills that were not reduced ($1,163) (p  
< .001). Most health care providers willing to reduce the amount of money 
owed for care received do so on the basis of charity care program policies that 
consider patients’ income. However, employees’ salary range was unrelated to 
receiving a bill reduction nor was there a statistically significant difference in 
the likelihood a bill would be reduced based on whether it was current or 
delinquent. However, 69% of bills in collection were reduced compared to only 
30% of bills not in collections (p < .001).
Employee health care experiences and financial circumstances
Employees who registered for MedPut and completed a survey (N = 263) gave 
responses concerning their experiences with medical bills, concerns about health 
care costs, and their financial circumstances. MedPut users were worse off 
financially and were more likely to have put off health care due to cost concerns 
compared to employees who registered for but did not use MedPut. Over half 
(52.46%) and nearly half (45.76%) of users said they had problems paying 
a medical bill and were contacted by a collections agency within the prior 12  
months, compared to 18.55% and 19.49% of non-users (p < .001), respectively. 
Users were also more likely to put off medical or dental care (63.33%) and put off 
filling a prescription (26.67%) due to cost concerns compared to 32.79% (p  
< .001) and 11.48% (p < .01) of non-users, respectively. Employee health care 
experiences and financial circumstances are presented in Table 4.
Discussion
This study examined the characteristics and experiences of employees enrolled 
in HDHPs across 21 companies who were offered MedPut, which allows 
employees to borrow money to pay health care bills and repay amounts 
borrowed through automatic payroll deductions. Regarding our first research 
question, having dependents was a significant predictor of both MedPut 
Table 4. Healthcare and financial circumstances of MedPut users and non-users.
Total %
Users %
Non-Users %
In the past 12 months: 
Problems paying a medical bill*** 
Contacted by collections agency***
29.73 
28.25
52.46 
45.76
18.55 
19.49
Good financial situationa
70.49
62.30
74.59
In the past 6 months:
Lost a job or had pay cut due to illness or injury
9.89
13.33
8.20
Put off medical or dental care due to cost***
42.86
63.33
32.79
Put off getting a prescription filled due to cost**
16.48
26.67
11.48
Note: N = 185 (n = 61 users and n = 124 non-users). 
aLive comfortably or able to meet basic expenses with a little left over for extras. 
** p < .01, *** p < .001
100
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

registration and use. This finding suggests that employees with family cover­
age may have cash flow challenges in meeting their deductibles, as plan 
benefits for any family member do not begin until the full family deductible 
is met, with certain exceptions like annual physicals. We also found that 
employees in small companies were more likely to register for MedPut. The 
combination of these two findings may reflect that 31% of workers in small 
companies pay greater than 50% of family premiums compared to 7% among 
workers in larger companies (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022b). Individuals 
are more likely to have medical debt if they have dependents. In addition, if 
premiums are taking up quite a bit of a person’s income, they may experience 
a greater challenge in paying their medical debt.
In answering the second research question, the vast majority of bills sub­
mitted to be paid by MedPut were for delinquent accounts, a fifth of which were 
in collections status. This finding indicates that employees who used MedPut 
faced a double-bind of paying for health care out-of-pocket until they reached 
their deductible and dealing with existing medical debt. That over a third of bills 
were reduced through negotiation with health care providers and collection 
agencies suggests that employees may have more leverage than they realize in 
getting portions of owed amounts forgiven. Accounts in collection were more 
than twice as likely to be reduced than current and delinquent accounts.
Lastly, MedPut appeared to reach a group of employees who struggle with 
paying for health care and are prone to delay seeking health care because of 
cost concerns. This finding is important because prior research indicates that 
individuals with HDHPs – including those with chronic illnesses such as 
cancer – are more likely than those without HDHPs to delay seeking health 
care (Al Rowas et al., 2017; Wharam et al., 2018, Wharam et al., 2019). In turn, 
delayed care is associated with poor health outcomes (Kraft et al., 2009; 
Prentice & Pizer, 2007). Many financial products and services available to 
patients may not relieve them of medical debt or improve healthcare use rates. 
Several researchers demonstrated the negative financial and health outcomes 
patients experienced due to high OOP costs for health care services (Kalousova 
& Burgard, 2013; Peter et al., 2016; Seymour, 2020). Associations between 
medical debt and health care use reveal individuals who have more medical 
debt are more likely to avoid seeking health care services to the detriment of 
their health (Bennett & Dismuke, 2010; Doty et al., 2005; Wiltshire et al., 2016; 
Zhang et al., 2018).
Study strengths and limitations
This study is one of the few to examine the impact of a fintech application 
(MedPut) on employees’ medical expenses. However, several limitations exist. 
First, only one cohort was included in this study and adding more cohorts may 
help to diversify the sample. Second, data was collected twice, however, only 35 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
101

employees completed both surveys. The low rate of survey completion limits our 
ability to conduct any longitudinal analyses. Future studies could offer an 
incentive to increase the rate of employee participation in the study over time. 
Third, no data on employee health status was collected, which may impact the 
internal validity of the study. Wharam et al. (2018) found that patients suffering 
from a chronic illness (macrovascular diabetes) were more likely to delay seeking 
health care because of costs than those who did not have a chronic illness. 
Similarly, employees in this study who have a chronic illness may be more likely 
to delay seeking health care due to costs than employees who do not have 
a chronic illness. Fourth, we did not have detailed information about employees’ 
health plans. Our results may have been different had we accounted for elements 
of patient cost share such as deductible amounts and coinsurance rates.
Implications for practice, policy and research
Fintech may make it easier for people to access financial products and services 
that better meet their needs than traditional banking. MedPut provided a source 
of affordable credit to workers who may have lacked better options for getting 
caught up on their medical bills. Yet fintech cannot solve the financial problems 
individuals and families face due to low wages, unaffordable housing, healthcare, 
and childcare, and a lack of social protection (Friedline, 2020). For example, 
a popular fintech app in the workplace is earned wage access (EWA), where 
workers can access funds in advance of payday to help pay bills or meet other 
needs (Despard et al., 2020a). Yet EWA would mostly be unnecessary if employ­
ers paid living wages (Despard et al., 2020b) and workers had paid leave, 
universal childcare, affordable healthcare and other social protections.
From an employer perspective, the $2 to $6 monthly fee is a modest cost to 
offer MedPut as a benefit for employees enrolled in HDHPs to pay for 
healthcare while in their deductible periods. Yet we found that MedPut was 
used mostly to pay for overdue medical bills. More impactful ways for employ­
ers to help employees afford healthcare are to offer insurance plans with lower 
deductibles, offer wage-based premiums (i.e., lower-paid employees pay lower 
premiums) and fund employee HSAs for the deductible amount so employees 
can use money in the HSA to pay for care while under the deductible. But 
employers may be unwilling or unable to pay for these more expensive 
solutions, which suggests the need for changes in public policies concerning 
health coverage as we outline below.
That over a third of bills submitted to MedPut were reduced through 
negotiation suggests that hospitals and other health care providers could do 
a better job of offering charity care discounts (Velasquez, 2021) and zero- 
interest payment plans to patients, especially when patients with HDHPs are 
in their deductible periods. Nearly half of U.S. employees are low-wage earners 
whose median hourly wages fall well within income guidelines for most charity 
102
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

care programs (Ross et al., 2020). Yet charity care accounts for less than 2% of 
expenses among hospitals (Zare et al., 2021). ACA provisions under 
Section 501(r)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code could be strengthened with 
respect to nonprofit hospitals’ required reporting on Schedule H of the Form 
990. Social workers in hospital settings are ideally situated to assist screening 
patients for financial assistance eligibility, especially for patients in need of 
specialty care which is harder for uninsured and underinsured patients to 
access (Hall et al., 2013).
A policy option for increasing health insurance affordability among 
employees is to allow employees to opt-in to Affordable Care Act (ACA) 
marketplace plans with subsidies. Currently, if an employer’s plan meets 
ACA affordability and coverage criteria, employees are ineligible for market­
place subsidies. Under the Federal Poverty Level safe harbor rule of the ACA, 
employer-sponsored coverage is considered affordable if at least one plan 
offering does not exceed $103.14 per month in premiums for single coverage 
(Brandon, 2021). It is vital for social workers to advocate to change policies 
that do not allow marketplace subsidies for financially vulnerable populations.
Premiums are only one component of employees’ cost share and 
many employees need family coverage which is much more expensive. 
The inability of employees to opt-in to ACA marketplace subsidies is 
particularly problematic for employees entering or reentering the work­
force who experience lost Medicaid eligibility ($0 premium) and assume 
employer coverage ($500 premium, on average). Social workers can 
advocate for longer-term and less administratively complex policy solu­
tions that allow individuals and families to opt-in to Medicare coverage, 
raising income eligibility thresholds for Medicaid, and making Medicaid 
a federal program only which provides states budgetary relief and 
protection against the volatility of health care expenditures (Brandon,  
2021).
It is important to consider the implications of these types of policy changes 
with respect to the COVID-19 pandemic. Workers in our study already strug­
gling to pay medical bills might have been reluctant to seek care if they had been 
exposed to the coronavirus (especially in customer-facing industries) and/or 
decided to still come to work to avoid losing pay, potentially putting themselves 
and their coworkers at risk. Nearly a third (29%) of workers with household 
income below $40,000 said they went to work with COVID-19 symptoms 
because they could not afford to lose pay (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022a), 
suggesting the need for social workers to advocate for universal paid sick leave. 
Also, households in which a member lost a job at some point during the 
pandemic were nearly four times more likely to have problems affording medical 
care or prescription drugs compared to households with no job loss (Despard et 
al., 2021). Having the opportunity to opt-in to ACA Marketplace subsidies or 
Medicare would give workers uninterrupted health coverage in the event of job 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
103

loss. More research on this topic from the social work perspective is needed to 
protect individuals and families struggling to pay for medical expenses.
Conclusion
We illustrate how a well-intended effort to provide a technological solution to 
help employees enrolled in HDHPs pay for their healthcare expenses illumi­
nated problems of underinsurance. Rather than solely focusing on increasing 
the overall rate of insurance, a standard of affordability should be applied that 
includes individuals and families’ full cost share relative to their incomes and 
used to help assess the sufficiency of employer, health care provider, and 
public policies. Social workers are in an ideal position to advocate for these 
health care policy changes to protect financially vulnerable populations. 
Examples of advocacy activities include organizing meetings and educating 
health care staff, patients, and families about patient eligibility for charity care 
and creating petitions to improve the advertisement of charity care.
Note
1. A case included one or more bills.
Disclosure statement
No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).
Funding
The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

Sally A. Hageman , PhD, MSW 
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-0694-9829
